claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 12 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 12 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 12 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit! Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for.

**How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?**

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, we can go through each one, but let's start with one child first. Please tell me their age (or age range from the options above), and if you'd like, you can share their name so I can keep track.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have two children actually - Alex who is 12, and Blake who is 8. They both live with me here at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Wonderful, thank you for that information! It's great that you've told me about both children upfront. Let me work through the eligibility assessment for Alex (12) and Blake (8).

Since both children are **under 16 years old**, they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit. Let me continue with the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about their work status:

**Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (12 and 8), I expect they're not working, but I just need to confirm this to proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all - they're just kids! Alex is in school and Blake is too. They don't have any jobs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That makes perfect sense - they're both in school! So yes, neither Alex nor Blake are working 24+ hours per week. Let me continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits like Employment and Support Allowance (ESA) or Universal Credit. Given their ages, this would be very unusual, but I need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they don't receive any benefits themselves. They're just children - I'm the one who handles all that sort of thing for the family.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly as I'd expect! Since Alex and Blake don't receive any benefits in their own right, let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now about apprenticeships:

**Are Alex and Blake in any type of apprenticeship?**

Since you mentioned they're both in school, I'm guessing they're not in apprenticeships, but just want to confirm. (This question is more relevant for older teens, but we need to check for completeness.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are in any apprenticeships. Like I said, Alex is 12 and Blake is 8 - they're just in regular school!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Absolutely, that makes complete sense! They're both just in regular school, so no apprenticeship concerns. Let me move on to the next part of the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about your residency status. You mentioned the children live with you "at home" - I just need to confirm:

**Do you normally live in the UK?**

(There are several ways to qualify for residency purposes, but the most common is simply living in the UK. If you have a different situation - such as settled or pre-settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme, or you're a Crown servant abroad - please let me know.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. We're all here in England.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, thank you! Since you normally live in the UK, you meet the residency requirements. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now about your responsibility for the children:

**Are you responsible for Alex and Blake?**

You've already mentioned they both live with you at home, which means you meet this requirement. Just to confirm - they live with you, is that correct?

(For reference, you can also qualify if you contribute to their upkeep financially, even if they don't live with you - but living with them is the most straightforward way to meet this requirement.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right - they both live with me at home. I take care of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that confirms you're responsible for both Alex and Blake. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example, does the children's other parent or another family member claim Child Benefit for either of them? 

- If no one else is claiming, that's straightforward
- If someone else is claiming but you've agreed that you should be the one to claim instead, that's also fine
- If there's a dispute about who should claim, HMRC would need to make the final decision

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for them. It's just me looking after them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's straightforward then. Let's proceed with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now about where Alex and Blake are currently living:

**Are Alex and Blake living at home with you under normal circumstances?**

Or are either of them currently:
- In hospital
- In local authority care
- Being fostered

From what you've told me, it sounds like they're both living at home with you normally, but I just need to confirm.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they're both living at home with me under normal circumstances. Neither of them are in hospital, in care, or being fostered - they're just regular kids living at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Both children living at home under normal circumstances. Let me continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is it correct that the local council is NOT paying towards Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, you're not receiving any local council payments specifically for housing or looking after the children.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. I'm supporting them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful news! Based on all the information you've provided, I can now give you the outcome of your Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary

| Child | Age | Eligible? |
|-------|-----|-----------|
| Alex | 12 | ✅ Yes |
| Blake | 8 | ✅ Yes |

### Why You Qualify

Both children meet all the eligibility requirements:
- ✅ Under 16 years old
- ✅ Not working 24+ hours per week
- ✅ Not receiving benefits in their own right
- ✅ Not in a disqualifying apprenticeship
- ✅ You live in the UK
- ✅ You're responsible for them (they live with you)
- ✅ No one else is claiming for them
- ✅ Local council is not paying for their care

### Next Steps to Claim

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** for Alex and Blake

### Important Note 💡

If you or your partner earns over **£60,000** per year, you may be affected by the **High Income Child Benefit Charge**. You can still claim, but some or all of it may need to be repaid through your tax return.

### Payment Information

Payments are usually made **every 4 weeks**. If you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits, you may be able to receive payments weekly.

👉 **Apply here:** [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 12) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They do not receive benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Their parent/guardian lives in the UK; (6) Their parent/guardian is responsible for them as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them; (8) They are living at home under normal circumstances; (9) The local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 8) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They do not receive benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Their parent/guardian lives in the UK; (6) Their parent/guardian is responsible for them as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them; (8) They are living at home under normal circumstances; (9) The local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They live in the UK, both children are under 16 and living at home under normal circumstances, neither child is working or in an apprenticeship, no one else is claiming for them, and the local council is not contributing to their care. All eligibility criteria are met for both Alex and Blake.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements

Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 12) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They do not receive benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Their parent/guardian lives in the UK; (6) Their parent/guardian is responsible for them as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them; (8) They are living at home under normal circumstances; (9) The local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (age 8) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) They are under 16 years old, meeting the age requirement automatically; (2) They are not working 24+ hours per week; (3) They do not receive benefits in their own right; (4) They are not in a disqualifying apprenticeship; (5) Their parent/guardian lives in the UK; (6) Their parent/guardian is responsible for them as they live together; (7) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for them; (8) They are living at home under normal circumstances; (9) The local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. They live in the UK, both children are under 16 and living at home under normal circumstances, neither child is working or in an apprenticeship, no one else is claiming for them, and the local council is not contributing to their care.
